parts.prompt:
[Option A] Global cross-cultural interactions, including the transfer of knowledge, scientific learning, and technological
developments, increased throughout the period 1200 to 1750.
Develop an argument that evaluates the extent to which ongoing cross-cultural interactions affected trade and/or
exploration during the period circa 1450 to 1750.
parts.scoring_notes: Student selects ONE option. Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence (2), Analysis & Reasoning (2). Total: 6 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible claim about the extent to which cross-cultural interactions affected trade and/or exploration in circa 1450–1750. Must evaluate degree and establish a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Evaluative claim about extent of cross-cultural interactions' effect on trade or exploration
Line of reasoning explaining the mechanism or type of interaction
alternatives.correct_example: Cross-cultural interactions were the primary driver of European maritime exploration in the period 1450–1750, as the transfer of Islamic navigational knowledge (astrolabe, lateen sail, astronomical charts) and Chinese technologies (magnetic compass, stern rudder) to Europe directly enabled the voyages that opened Atlantic and Indian Ocean trade routes — demonstrating that European exploration was less a product of European innovation than of accumulated cross-cultural technology transfer.

required_elements: Thesis arguing that cross-cultural interactions had differential effects on different types of trade
alternatives.correct_example: While cross-cultural interactions transformed overland Silk Road trade by introducing diasporic merchant communities (Jewish, Armenian, Sogdian, Indian traders) who reduced transaction costs by providing credit networks and shared legal frameworks, the effect on maritime trade was even more fundamental — cross-cultural transfer of navigational technology and geographic knowledge enabled a qualitative shift from coastal to open-ocean sailing that created the first truly global trade network.

wrong_examples: Cross-cultural interactions affected trade in many ways — restates the prompt without an evaluative argument.
Trade was important in this period — too vague, no claim about the role of cross-cultural interactions.
common_traps: Students often write about trade without connecting it to cross-cultural interactions — the thesis must specifically address how cultural exchange shaped trade/exploration.
The period is 1450–1750 but the context provided mentions 1200–1750 — evidence from before 1450 is appropriate as context but the main argument should focus on 1450–1750.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe broader historical context for cross-cultural interactions and their role in enabling trade/exploration 1450–1750. Must be substantive and connected to the argument.

required_elements: Broader context about pre-1450 cross-cultural exchange networks that set the stage for the period
Explains how that prior context enabled or shaped the 1450–1750 developments
alternatives.correct_example: The Islamic Golden Age (8th–13th centuries) created a vast zone of cultural and commercial exchange stretching from Spain to Southeast Asia, in which scholars, merchants, and travelers circulated knowledge across political and linguistic boundaries. Islamic scholars preserved and advanced Greek scientific knowledge, developed algebra and trigonometry, and produced geographic works (Ibn Battuta, al-Idrisi) that mapped the known world. This intellectual and commercial heritage — transmitted to Europe through Andalusia (especially Toledo, where Arabic texts were translated into Latin) and through Crusader contact with the Eastern Mediterranean — provided the navigational and geographical knowledge that made European oceanic exploration possible after 1450.

wrong_examples: Trade existed before this period — too vague, does not explain the specific cross-cultural exchange networks that shaped 1450–1750 developments.
The Silk Roads connected Asia and Europe — acknowledges prior trade without explaining how those networks enabled the specific changes of 1450–1750.
common_traps: Contextualization must explain the connection to the argument, not just state a prior fact — the student needs to show why the prior context matters for understanding the essay's argument.
The Mongol period (1200–1350) is excellent context because it demonstrates how political unification accelerated cross-cultural exchange, creating the conditions that the 1450–1750 period built upon.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (c1): Provide at least two specific, accurate pieces of evidence about cross-cultural interactions affecting trade or exploration in 1450–1750.

required_elements: At least two specific pieces of accurate evidence
Evidence must be specifically about cross-cultural interaction affecting trade or exploration in the period
alternatives.correct_example: Two pieces of evidence: (1) Vasco da Gama's 1497–1499 voyage to India succeeded largely because he hired Ahmad ibn Majid, an Arab navigator, to guide the fleet across the Indian Ocean from Malindi (East Africa) — demonstrating that Portuguese exploration depended directly on Islamic navigational expertise. (2) The Portuguese adoption of the lateen sail (triangular sail from Arab dhow design) allowed ships to sail closer to the wind, enabling the tacking maneuvers necessary for Atlantic voyaging — a direct technological transfer from cross-cultural contact in the Mediterranean and along African coasts.

required_elements: Evidence about how Chinese trading diaspora networks shaped Southeast Asian commerce
alternatives.correct_example: Evidence about cross-cultural trade networks: (1) Chinese merchant diasporas (Hokkien and Cantonese traders) established commercial communities throughout Southeast Asia — Malacca, Manila, Batavia — that served as intermediaries between European trading companies and local markets, demonstrating how cross-cultural commercial networks were essential infrastructure for European expansion in Asia. (2) The Columbian Exchange itself was a product of cross-cultural contact: the transfer of American crops (maize, potatoes, sweet potatoes) to Asia and Africa transformed agricultural productivity in those regions, reshaping demographic and commercial patterns in ways that fed back into global trade volumes.

wrong_examples: Europeans explored new trade routes — too vague, does not specify the cross-cultural interaction component.
The Portuguese traded with Asia — not specific enough to count as evidence.
common_traps: Evidence for this prompt must explicitly involve cross-cultural exchange — not just describe trade activity that happened to involve multiple cultures.
Technological transfer (navigational tools, crop exchanges) is excellent evidence that directly links cross-cultural interaction to trade/exploration outcomes.

scoring_points.description: Evidence+ (c2): Use specific evidence to explicitly support the argument about the extent to which cross-cultural interactions affected trade/exploration.

required_elements: Specific evidence explicitly connected to the evaluative argument
Explains how evidence supports the claim about extent or mechanism
alternatives.correct_example: The creation of the Manila Galleon trade (1565–1815) illustrates how cross-cultural interactions produced fundamentally new trade networks: Spanish traders in Manila exchanged American silver (from Potosí mines) for Chinese silk and porcelain brought by Chinese junks to Manila — a trade that only existed because of Spanish imperial expansion (itself enabled by cross-cultural navigational technology transfer), Chinese commercial diaspora networks, and American indigenous silver-mining knowledge. This supports the argument that cross-cultural interactions were not merely enabling conditions but constitutive elements of the new global trading system, without which the specific commodity flows of the period would have been impossible.

wrong_examples: This shows that cross-cultural trade was important — does not connect evidence to a specific claim about extent.
The Portuguese hired Arab navigators — identifies evidence without arguing what it proves about the extent of cross-cultural interactions' effect.
common_traps: The c2 point requires the student to explicitly state how the evidence proves their claim — do not assume the grader will make the connection.
Evidence that shows the LIMITS of cross-cultural interactions' effect can also earn c2 if it is used to qualify the argument.

scoring_points.description: Historical Reasoning: Use comparison, causation, or CCOT to frame or structure the argument about cross-cultural interactions' effect on trade/exploration.

required_elements: Explicit and consistent application of a historical reasoning skill
Applied to structure the argument about extent
alternatives.correct_example: CAUSATION: The fall of Constantinople to the Ottomans in 1453 caused a shift in European trade strategy by threatening the overland Silk Road route and raising the cost of Eastern Mediterranean transit trade. This prompted Portuguese and Spanish investment in Atlantic maritime routes as an alternative, which in turn caused the transfer of navigational resources (cartographic knowledge, ship design, navigational instruments) from Mediterranean to Atlantic contexts. The causal chain runs from political disruption → economic motivation → cross-cultural technology transfer → new exploration — demonstrating that cross-cultural interactions were not self-generating but were caused by specific political and economic conditions.

required_elements: CCOT showing what changed and what continued in cross-cultural trade patterns 1200–1750
alternatives.correct_example: CCOT: Before 1450, cross-cultural trade interactions in the Indian Ocean operated through an established system of monsoon-based seasonal trade with Arab, Indian, Malay, and Chinese merchants circulating through established port cities (Kilwa, Calicut, Malacca, Quanzhou). After 1450, Portuguese gunpowder force and the creation of the Estado da India (Portuguese Asian trading empire) disrupted but did not fully replace this system — what changed was the introduction of European armed trading; what continued was the fundamental role of non-European merchant communities who remained the dominant carriers of most Asian trade volume even within the Portuguese system.

wrong_examples: Cross-cultural interactions caused new trade — names causation without explaining the mechanism.
Things changed over time — mentions CCOT without specifying what changed, what continued, and why.
common_traps: Students often describe a sequence of events and label it causation — the skill requires explaining the mechanism that connects cause and effect.
CCOT is particularly effective for this prompt because the 1200–1750 framing explicitly invites tracing patterns over time.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate complex understanding of the extent to which cross-cultural interactions affected trade/exploration in 1450–1750. Must be sustained throughout the essay.

required_elements: Sustained complex argumentation that qualifies, complicates, or corroborates the argument
Demonstrates understanding of tensions, contradictions, or broader significance
alternatives.correct_example: A complex account must acknowledge the paradox that European powers, who benefited most from cross-cultural technology transfer in the short term, subsequently attempted to monopolize trade routes and restrict cross-cultural exchange through systems like the Portuguese cartaz (trade license) system in the Indian Ocean, which forced non-European merchants to pay for Portuguese protection (or face attack). The very success of cross-cultural interaction in enabling European expansion created incentives for Europeans to disrupt the open cross-cultural exchange networks that had made their expansion possible — demonstrating that the relationship between cross-cultural interaction and trade was historically contingent, not simply progressive or linear.

wrong_examples: Cross-cultural interactions had both positive and negative effects — states complexity without developing it.
In conclusion, this shows how trade was affected — generic statement without specific historical content.
common_traps: For this prompt, excellent complexity often involves examining HOW European powers appropriated cross-cultural knowledge while simultaneously attempting to restrict it for competitive advantage.
Connecting to the broader Columbian Exchange and its global demographic consequences adds strong complexity by showing unexpected or unintended effects of cross-cultural interaction.

parts.prompt:
[Option B] In the period circa 1750 to 1900, revolutions and independence movements such as the American Revolution,
the French Revolution, the Haitian Revolution, and Latin American independence movements occurred around
the world.
Develop an argument that evaluates the extent to which Enlightenment ideas were the primary cause of one of
the revolutions or independence movements (other than the American Revolution) from the period circa 1750
to 1900.
parts.scoring_notes: Student selects ONE option. Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence (2), Analysis & Reasoning (2). Total: 6 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible claim about the extent to which Enlightenment ideas were the PRIMARY cause of a specific revolution or independence movement (not the American Revolution). Must evaluate degree and establish a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Names a specific revolution or independence movement (not American Revolution)
Evaluative claim about whether Enlightenment ideas were the PRIMARY cause (extent)
Line of reasoning either supporting or qualifying the primacy claim
alternatives.correct_example: While Enlightenment concepts of natural rights and popular sovereignty were clearly present in the rhetoric of the Haitian Revolution (1791–1804), the primary causes were the specific colonial conditions of Saint-Domingue — the brutal treatment of enslaved people, the contradictions of a 'liberty' that applied only to white colonists, and the outbreak of war between France, Britain, and Spain that created a military power vacuum. Enlightenment ideas were catalysts that gave the revolution a universal language, but the underlying causes were rooted in the material conditions of slavery and colonial exploitation.

required_elements: Thesis arguing Enlightenment ideas WERE the primary cause of the French Revolution
alternatives.correct_example: Enlightenment ideas — specifically Rousseau's concept of popular sovereignty, Voltaire's anticlerical rationalism, and Montesquieu's theory of separation of powers — were the primary intellectual framework that gave the French Revolution its radical character and distinguished it from earlier tax revolts or noble rebellions. While fiscal crisis and food shortages triggered the revolutionary moment, Enlightenment thought determined that the response would be a fundamental reordering of political legitimacy, not merely a demand for fiscal reform.

wrong_examples: Enlightenment ideas caused revolutions — too broad, no specific revolution named, no evaluation of 'primary' vs. other causes.
The French Revolution was caused by many factors — no evaluative claim about the primacy of Enlightenment ideas.
common_traps: The prompt specifically asks about PRIMARY cause — the thesis must take a position on whether Enlightenment ideas were the most important factor, not just acknowledge they were present.
Students cannot use the American Revolution — this is an explicit constraint in the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe broader historical context for the spread of Enlightenment ideas and the revolutionary period 1750–1900. Must be substantive and connected to the argument.

required_elements: Broader context about the intellectual, economic, or political conditions that produced Enlightenment thought
Connected to why Enlightenment ideas had revolutionary potential in this period
alternatives.correct_example: The Enlightenment emerged from the Scientific Revolution of the 17th century, which established that natural laws — not divine will — governed physical phenomena. Enlightenment philosophers (Locke, Rousseau, Voltaire, Montesquieu) applied this naturalistic framework to human society, arguing that political institutions should be rationally designed to maximize human well-being rather than simply accepted as God-given. This represented a fundamental departure from divine right monarchy and religious authority as bases of political legitimacy, creating an intellectual toolkit that challengers to existing power could use to argue that revolutionary change was not merely rebellion but rational progress — providing essential context for why Enlightenment ideas had such transformative political potential when the material conditions for revolution emerged.

required_elements: Context about the global circulation of Enlightenment texts and their adaptation in colonial contexts
alternatives.correct_example: The Atlantic World of the 18th century created conditions for unprecedented circulation of ideas through print media — newspapers, pamphlets, and books crossed the Atlantic and circulated ideas from Paris, London, and Philadelphia to colonial ports in the Caribbean and Latin America. Educated elites in Saint-Domingue, Buenos Aires, and Caracas read Enlightenment texts and debated their implications in coffeehouses and salons. This intellectual cosmopolitanism meant that Enlightenment ideas reached revolutionary leaders across the Atlantic simultaneously, creating a shared intellectual framework for multiple independence movements — but also reaching enslaved and free colored populations (like Haiti's Toussaint Louverture) in ways that fundamentally challenged the racial hierarchy the original Enlightenment thinkers had often left unquestioned.

wrong_examples: Enlightenment philosophers had ideas about rights — restates the prompt premise without explaining the broader historical conditions.
Before this period, monarchies ruled Europe — too vague, not connected to how Enlightenment ideas became revolutionary catalysts.
common_traps: Strong contextualization for this prompt explains WHY Enlightenment ideas had particular revolutionary force in this specific period — connecting to the print revolution, Atlantic commercial networks, or colonial contradictions.
Students sometimes confuse context with evidence — the context should explain the broader intellectual and political landscape, not describe the specific revolution being argued.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (c1): Provide at least two specific, accurate pieces of evidence about Enlightenment ideas' role in the chosen revolution or independence movement.

required_elements: At least two specific, accurate pieces of evidence about the revolution and Enlightenment connections
Evidence must be specific: named documents, leaders, events, or ideological claims
alternatives.correct_example: Evidence for the Haitian Revolution: (1) Toussaint Louverture's 1801 Constitution declared universal human rights and abolished slavery — directly echoing Enlightenment natural rights language — but also granted Louverture life-long authoritarian power, demonstrating both the adoption and transformation of Enlightenment principles in a colonial context. (2) The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) created an ideological contradiction that enslaved people in Saint-Domingue exploited: if all men were born free and equal, French colonists could not simultaneously claim liberty from the crown and deny it to enslaved people on their plantations — demonstrating how Enlightenment rhetoric became a weapon in the hands of the enslaved.

required_elements: Evidence for Latin American independence movements and Enlightenment connections
alternatives.correct_example: Evidence for South American independence: (1) Simón Bolívar's Jamaica Letter (1815) explicitly invoked Enlightenment natural rights theory to justify American independence from Spanish colonial rule, arguing that peoples had a right to self-determination that could not be denied by hereditary monarchy. (2) The establishment of republics across Spanish America (Venezuela 1811, Argentina 1816, Mexico 1821) copied constitutional frameworks from Montesquieu and directly modeled governance structures on the separation of powers principle — demonstrating that Enlightenment political philosophy shaped not just the rhetoric but the institutional design of the new states.

wrong_examples: The Haitian Revolution was influenced by Enlightenment ideas — too vague, no specific evidence of how or which ideas.
Leaders read Enlightenment books — not specific enough to count as evidence.
common_traps: Evidence must show BOTH that Enlightenment ideas were present AND how they shaped the revolution — naming a philosopher without showing the connection to revolutionary events is insufficient.

Non-Enlightenment causes (material grievances, class conflicts, military power vacuums) can and should be mentioned as evidence that Enlightenment ideas were NOT the only cause — this supports a nuanced thesis.

scoring_points.description: Evidence+ (c2): Use specific evidence to explicitly support the argument about whether Enlightenment ideas were the primary cause of the chosen revolution.

required_elements: Evidence explicitly linked to the evaluative argument about primary causation
Explains how evidence supports or qualifies the primacy claim
alternatives.correct_example: The Haitian Revolution's causes illustrate why Enlightenment ideas cannot be considered the primary cause: the largest slave uprising in history (August 1791) began as a Vodou ceremony at Bois Caïman where leaders drew on African spiritual tradition — not Enlightenment philosophy — to mobilize the enslaved population. Most enslaved participants in the rebellion were African-born and illiterate in French, making direct exposure to Enlightenment texts impossible. This evidence supports the argument that material conditions (brutal slavery, racial hierarchy, military opportunity) were the primary causes, with Enlightenment ideas serving primarily as a justificatory language adopted by the educated mulatto and free Black leadership rather than as a generative force among the enslaved majority.

wrong_examples: This proves Enlightenment ideas were important — vague connection without explaining how evidence addresses the primacy claim.
Leaders used Enlightenment language — does not explain whether this makes Enlightenment ideas the PRIMARY cause.
common_traps: The 'primary cause' question is ideal for c2 because it forces argumentative reasoning — students need to explicitly weigh Enlightenment ideas against other causal factors.
Evidence showing NON-Enlightenment causes is often more powerful for a nuanced argument than simply accumulating evidence of Enlightenment influence.

scoring_points.description: Historical Reasoning: Use comparison, causation, or CCOT to frame or structure the argument about Enlightenment ideas' role in the chosen revolution.

required_elements: Explicit and consistent application of a historical reasoning skill
Applied to structure the argument about causation/extent
alternatives.correct_example: CAUSATION: The Haitian Revolution's causes can be analyzed through a multi-causal framework: (1) Immediate trigger — the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) created an ideological contradiction that mulatto free people of color (led by Vincent Ogé) used to demand political rights; (2) Structural cause — the extreme brutality of Saint-Domingue's plantation system (highest death rate in the Atlantic) created a population with nothing to lose; (3) Contingent cause — the outbreak of war between France, Britain, and Spain (1793) provided the military power vacuum that allowed the enslaved uprising to succeed militarily. Enlightenment ideas were the first (immediate trigger) in this causal chain, but the structural and contingent causes explain why the revolution succeeded when similar ideological contradictions did not produce revolution in other colonies.

required_elements: COMPARISON: Comparing two revolutions to determine what role Enlightenment ideas played
alternatives.correct_example: COMPARISON: Comparing the French Revolution and the Haitian Revolution reveals that Enlightenment ideas played a different causal role in each: in France, Enlightenment philosophy directly shaped the ideology of the revolutionary bourgeoisie who had access to texts and salons; in Haiti, Enlightenment rhetoric was adopted secondarily by educated free people of color while the enslaved majority was motivated primarily by freedom from bondage. The comparison demonstrates that Enlightenment ideas functioned as a necessary but insufficient cause — revolutions required material conditions of grievance AND an available ideological framework to express those grievances.

wrong_examples: Enlightenment ideas caused the revolution because people had new ideas — circular reasoning that does not explain the causal mechanism.
We can compare these revolutions — names comparison without developing what the comparison reveals.
common_traps: For causation: students need to explain WHY Enlightenment ideas had revolutionary force in this specific context — why in this period, why this population, why this revolution.
Comparison between two revolutions is highly effective for this prompt because it reveals what was specific about Enlightenment influence in each case.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate complex understanding of the extent to which Enlightenment ideas were the primary cause of the chosen revolution. Must be sustained throughout the essay.

required_elements: Sustained complex argumentation that qualifies, complicates, or corroborates the argument
Goes beyond simple 'Enlightenment caused/did not cause' to demonstrate sophisticated understanding
alternatives.correct_example: A complex account of the Haitian Revolution must acknowledge that Enlightenment ideas were both used AND transformed by the revolution: Toussaint Louverture and Jean-Jacques Dessalines adopted Enlightenment language of universal rights but extended those rights to include enslaved Africans — a position the original Enlightenment philosophers (who often held enslaved people or invested in plantation colonies) had not themselves taken. The Haitian Revolution thus represents a case where colonized people radicalized Enlightenment universalism beyond what its European authors intended, demonstrating that the relationship between Enlightenment ideas and revolution was not one of simple transmission but of creative appropriation and transformation. This complicates the 'extent' question: Enlightenment ideas influenced the revolution profoundly, but the revolution also changed those ideas.

wrong_examples: The revolution had both Enlightenment and non-Enlightenment causes — states complexity without developing it.
Enlightenment was important but other things mattered too — acknowledges multiple causes without explaining their relationship.
common_traps: The most effective complexity for this prompt involves showing how colonized peoples transformed Enlightenment ideas rather than simply receiving them — demonstrating historical agency and the two-way nature of intellectual exchange.

Connecting the Haitian Revolution's success to its influence on other independence movements (inspiring Bolívar, frightening slaveholders in the American South) adds strong complexity by showing its broader historical significance.

parts.prompt:
[Option C] In the twentieth century, revolutionary movements were inspired by a variety of ideologies, including
communism, nationalism, and/or religious ideas.
Develop an argument that evaluates the extent to which a twentieth-century revolutionary movement was
inspired by one of these ideologies.
parts.scoring_notes: Student selects ONE option. Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence (2), Analysis & Reasoning (2). Total: 6 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible claim about the extent to which a specific twentieth-century revolutionary movement was inspired by a specific ideology (communism, nationalism, or religious ideas). Must name the movement, name the ideology, and make an evaluative claim about extent.

required_elements: Names a specific revolutionary movement and a specific ideology
Evaluative claim about extent (how significantly or primarily the ideology inspired the movement)
Line of reasoning explaining how the ideology shaped the movement's goals, methods, or success
alternatives.correct_example: The Chinese Communist Revolution (1949) was deeply inspired by communist ideology — specifically Mao Zedong's adaptation of Marxism-Leninism to Chinese agrarian conditions — but its success depended equally on nationalist sentiment against Japanese imperialism and Western imperialism, suggesting that communism was the organizational framework while nationalism provided the mass mobilization base that made the revolution viable.

required_elements: Thesis arguing religious ideology was primary inspiration for a specific 20th-century revolution
alternatives.correct_example: The Iranian Revolution of 1979 was primarily inspired by Shi'a Islamic ideology as articulated by Ayatollah Khomeini's doctrine of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the Islamic jurist), which provided not only the revolutionary motivation but also the institutional blueprint for the Islamic Republic — making Iran distinct from other 20th-century revolutions where religious ideas supplemented rather than dominated the revolutionary framework.

wrong_examples: Revolutionary movements were inspired by ideologies — restates the prompt without naming a specific movement or making an evaluative claim.
Communism inspired many revolutions — too broad, no specific movement analyzed.
common_traps: Students must choose ONE ideology and argue its role — attempting to argue that communism AND nationalism AND religion equally inspired a movement will result in no clear evaluative claim.
The argument about EXTENT requires nuance — arguing the ideology was important but also had other contributing factors is typically more defensible than claiming it was the sole cause.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe broader historical context for the emergence of the chosen ideology as a revolutionary force in the 20th century. Must be substantive and connected to the argument.

required_elements: Broader context explaining why the chosen ideology became powerful in the 20th century
Connected to the specific revolutionary movement being argued
alternatives.correct_example: Marxism emerged in the mid-19th century as a critique of industrial capitalism's class inequalities, but it was Lenin's 1917 Russian Revolution that transformed it from a theoretical framework into a proven model for political revolution. The success of the Bolshevik Revolution demonstrated that a disciplined vanguard party could seize state power even in a predominantly agricultural country, contradicting Marx's prediction that communist revolutions would first occur in advanced industrial economies. This Leninist adaptation — that revolution could be organized from above by a party rather than requiring spontaneous mass uprising — became the template that Communist Parties across Asia, Africa, and Latin America would follow for the rest of the 20th century, providing essential context for why communist-inspired revolutions occurred in China, Vietnam, Cuba, and elsewhere.

required_elements: Context about colonialism and nationalism as background for 20th-century revolutionary movements
alternatives.correct_example: The 19th-century creation of European colonial empires through the Scramble for Africa (1880s) and expansion in Asia produced conditions that generated intense nationalist responses in the 20th century. Colonial rule imposed foreign administrative control, economic extraction, and racial hierarchy that delegitimized traditional authority structures and created educated colonial elites who had absorbed European nationalist ideas while experiencing their exclusion from political power. This structural contradiction — the colonizer teaching nationalism while denying it to the colonized — created the ideological foundation for 20th-century anti-colonial nationalist revolutions from Algeria to Vietnam to Kenya.

wrong_examples: There were revolutions in the 20th century — describes the period without explaining broader conditions.
Communism was developed by Marx and Engels — biographical context that doesn't explain why communist ideology had revolutionary force in the 20th century.
common_traps: Context should explain the conditions that made the chosen ideology available and compelling as a revolutionary framework — not just describe its intellectual history.
For communist revolutions: the Russian Revolution as context is highly effective because it directly influenced subsequent communist movements; explain the mechanism of influence.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (c1): Provide at least two specific, accurate pieces of evidence about the ideological inspiration for the chosen revolutionary movement.

required_elements: At least two specific pieces of accurate evidence about the movement's ideological foundations
Evidence must show specifically how the ideology shaped the movement
alternatives.correct_example: Evidence for the Chinese Communist Revolution: (1) Mao Zedong's 1927 'Report on an Investigation of the Peasant Movement in Hunan' adapted Marxist class analysis to Chinese conditions by arguing that peasants (not urban workers) were China's revolutionary class — a significant ideological innovation that shaped the Communists' mass-line strategy of rural base areas and guerrilla warfare. (2) The Long March (1934–1935), in which the Communist Party retreated 6,000 miles to survive KMT encirclement, demonstrated that communist ideology provided the organizational cohesion and ideological commitment that sustained the movement through military catastrophe — establishing Mao's authority and the Yan'an base that became the foundation for the 1949 victory.

required_elements: Evidence for the Vietnamese independence movement and the role of communist ideology
alternatives.correct_example: Evidence for the Vietnamese Revolution: (1) Ho Chi Minh's founding of the Viet Minh (League for the Independence of Vietnam, 1941) combined communist organizational structures with explicit Vietnamese nationalist goals — the organization's primary public objective was independence from France, not class revolution, demonstrating how communist ideology was adapted to nationalist purposes. (2) The Viet Cong's land redistribution program in areas under its control directly implemented communist agrarian policy (collective farming, expropriation of landlord holdings), demonstrating that ideology shaped concrete governance decisions, not just revolutionary rhetoric.

wrong_examples: Communists wanted to help the poor — too vague, no specific evidence about how ideology shaped a specific movement.
Mao was a communist leader — biographical statement without evidence connecting ideology to the revolution.
common_traps: Evidence must show specifically how ideology shaped the movement's goals, methods, or organization — not just state that the movement identified with an ideology.
Both pieces of evidence can come from the same movement; they should address different aspects of ideological influence (e.g., mass mobilization strategy + governance decisions).

scoring_points.description: Evidence+ (c2): Use specific evidence to explicitly support the argument about the extent to which the ideology inspired the revolutionary movement.

required_elements: Evidence explicitly connected to the evaluative claim about extent
Explains how evidence supports the argument about degree of ideological inspiration
alternatives.correct_example: The fact that the Chinese Communist Party adopted Marxist ideology but drastically reinterpreted it to fit Chinese agrarian conditions — rejecting Soviet advice at critical moments (Stalin recommended the CCP subordinate itself to the KMT; Mao rejected this after 1927) and developing distinctly Chinese models (rural base areas, mass-line politics, guerrilla warfare) — demonstrates that communist ideology was the essential framework but was significantly modified by pragmatic nationalist priorities. This evidence supports the argument that communist ideology inspired the revolution in fundamental ways (class analysis, party organization, anti-imperialism) while Chinese nationalism determined its specific content and strategies, making both ideologies necessary to explain the movement's character and success.

wrong_examples: This shows communism was important — vague statement without explaining what the evidence proves about extent of ideological inspiration.
The CCP used Marxism — identifies evidence without connecting it argumentatively to the extent question.
common_traps: c2 requires the student to explicitly argue what the evidence proves about the extent question — not just describe what the evidence shows.
Evidence of ideological ADAPTATION or modification is often more powerful than evidence of pure ideological adherence — it shows sophisticated understanding of how ideology functioned in context.

scoring_points.description: Historical Reasoning: Use comparison, causation, or CCOT to frame or structure the argument about the ideology's role in inspiring the revolutionary movement.

required_elements: Explicit and consistent application of a historical reasoning skill
Applied to structure the argument about the extent of ideological inspiration
alternatives.correct_example: COMPARISON: Comparing the Chinese Communist Revolution with the Cuban Revolution (1959) reveals different patterns of ideological inspiration: in China, communist ideology predated and shaped the organizational structure of the revolution from its origins in 1921; in Cuba, Fidel Castro's revolution was initially primarily nationalist (26th of July Movement) and Castro only formally embraced communist ideology after the revolution succeeded, partly in response to American hostility. This comparison suggests that communist ideology's role varied from primary organizing principle (China) to post-hoc justification (Cuba), demonstrating that the extent of ideological inspiration depended heavily on the specific political context of each revolution.

required_elements: CCOT: Tracing how the ideology's influence on the movement changed over time
alternatives.correct_example: CCOT: In the Iranian Revolution, religious ideology's role changed significantly over the 1979 revolutionary process: initially, the anti-Shah coalition included secular nationalists, communists, and Islamists united by opposition to the monarchy; after the Shah's departure, Khomeini systematically displaced secular and leftist allies and established Islamic governance as the sole revolutionary framework. What changed was the ideological composition of the ruling coalition; what continued was the religious framing of the revolution as a return to authentic Iranian identity against Western imperialism. This CCOT reveals that religious ideology became MORE dominant as the revolution progressed, not that it was the sole inspiration from the beginning.

wrong_examples: Ideology caused the revolution — names causation without explaining the mechanism.
Things changed during the revolution — mentions CCOT without specifying what changed, what continued, and why.
common_traps: Students often describe ideological content without applying a reasoning skill — the key is using the skill to advance the argument about EXTENT.
Comparison between two revolutions inspired by the same ideology is particularly effective for revealing what made ideological inspiration strong in some cases and weaker in others.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate complex understanding of the extent to which the chosen ideology inspired the revolutionary movement. Must be sustained throughout the essay.

required_elements: Sustained complex argumentation that qualifies, complicates, or corroborates the argument
Goes beyond simple 'ideology caused/did not cause' to demonstrate sophisticated understanding
alternatives.correct_example: A complex account of the Iranian Revolution must acknowledge the paradox that the revolution succeeded by combining Shi'a religious ideology with modern political technologies — Khomeini's cassette tapes circulated his sermons throughout Iran, and the mass demonstration tactic (derived from Gandhian nonviolent resistance) was used to mobilize millions, demonstrating that the revolution's ideological content (Islamic governance) was delivered through distinctly modern organizational methods. Furthermore, the revolution's subsequent trajectory — eliminating leftist and nationalist allies who had participated in overthrowing the Shah — reveals that the extent to which religious ideology 'inspired' the revolution was partly constructed retroactively through the exercise of power, not simply a pre-existing popular consensus. This complicates the idea that ideological inspiration has a simple relationship to revolutionary outcomes.

wrong_examples: Ideology was one of several factors — states multiple causes without explaining their relationship.
This shows how ideology shaped revolutions — generic statement without specific historical content.
common_traps: The most effective complexity for this prompt involves showing how a single revolutionary movement was inspired by MULTIPLE ideologies simultaneously, and how those ideologies interacted or conflicted.

Showing how the revolutionary movement TRANSFORMED the ideology (not just applied it) demonstrates the highest level of historical complexity — the revolution changed communism/nationalism/religious thought as much as those ideas shaped the revolution.
